_meta.authoringNotes: Clinical details cross-checked against 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Coronary Revascularization Guideline, 2019 ESC Guidelines on Chronic Coronary Syndromes, and ORBITA trial (PMID 28732069). Epidemiology from AHA Heart Disease Statistics 2024.

h1Title: Angina Pectoris: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Angina pectoris is chest discomfort caused by transient myocardial ischaemia when coronary blood flow fails to meet the heart's oxygen demands. Approximately 10 million people in the United States live with angina, and it represents the most common initial presentation of coronary artery disease. Stable angina produces predictable exertional discomfort that resolves within minutes with rest or sublingual nitrates; unstable angina occurs at rest or with minimal exertion and signals plaque instability requiring urgent evaluation. Left untreated, angina indicates a risk of acute MI of approximately 2-3% per year in stable disease and up to 20% within 30 days for unstable disease. Optimal medical therapy, coronary revascularisation, and risk factor control together reduce symptoms and improve prognosis.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 10 million US adults have angina; UK prevalence is approximately 2 million (BHF 2022)
keyStats.demographics: Stable angina prevalence approximately 5% in men and 3% in women aged 45-54, rising to 14-15% in both sexes by age 65-74
keyStats.avgAge: Symptom onset typically 45-65 years; average age of stable angina diagnosis approximately 63 years
keyStats.globalCases: Coronary artery disease affects over 200 million people worldwide (GBD 2022), with angina the most common symptomatic manifestation

definition: Angina pectoris (ICD-10: I20.9) is a clinical syndrome of episodic chest discomfort arising from temporary, reversible myocardial ischaemia without necrosis. The mechanism is a mismatch between myocardial oxygen supply and demand: fixed atherosclerotic coronary stenoses limit the increase in blood flow during exertion (stable angina), or dynamic changes in plaque, vasomotor tone, or thrombosis reduce resting flow (unstable angina and variant angina). The Canadian Cardiovascular Society (CCS) classifies stable angina into four grades based on the exertional threshold that provokes symptoms, from CCS I (symptoms only with strenuous activity) to CCS IV (symptoms at rest or with any activity). Unstable angina is defined by new-onset angina, accelerating angina, or rest angina with a normal or near-normal troponin — distinguishing it from NSTEMI, where troponin rises above the 99th-percentile limit. Microvascular angina (syndrome X) occurs when epicardial arteries appear normal on angiography but coronary microvascular dysfunction impairs perfusion reserve; it is increasingly recognised particularly in women. Vasospastic (Prinzmetal's) angina arises from transient focal coronary spasm causing rest pain and transient ST elevation. Angina is managed by cardiologists and general internists; refractory or complex cases require interventional cardiology or cardiac surgery.

typesClassification.description: Predictable exertional chest discomfort from fixed coronary stenosis. Resolves within 5-10 minutes at rest or with sublingual nitrate. CCS I-IV grading. Annual MI risk approximately 1-2% on optimal medical therapy.

New-onset, rest, or crescendo angina without troponin rise. Indicates plaque instability. Carries 5-10% risk of MI or death within 30 days without treatment. Requires urgent admission and anti-thrombotic therapy.

Focal coronary artery spasm causing rest pain with transient ST elevation reversible with nitrates or calcium channel blockers. Occurs predominantly at night or early morning. Triggered by cold, smoking, cocaine, and emotional stress.

Typical angina with normal epicardial arteries on angiography. Coronary flow reserve is reduced on invasive testing or cardiac MRI stress. More common in women and associated with hypertension, diabetes, and post-menopause.

Persistent symptoms for more than 3 months despite optimal medical therapy and revascularisation when technically feasible. Requires specialist input for spinal cord stimulation, enhanced external counterpulsation, or transmyocardial laser revascularisation.

primarySymptoms: Central chest tightness, pressure, heaviness, or burning lasting 2-10 minutes, typically brought on by physical exertion, cold air, heavy meals, or emotional stress and relieved by rest or sublingual nitrate within 5 minutes.
Radiation of the discomfort to the left arm, left shoulder, jaw, neck, or interscapular area, reflecting referred visceral pain from ischaemic myocardium via shared thoracic nerve pathways.
Breathlessness accompanying the chest discomfort, arising from transient diastolic dysfunction and elevated filling pressures during ischaemia.
Fatigue or heaviness in both arms with exertion in patients with extensive ischaemia, sometimes without any chest component (anginal equivalent).
Epigastric discomfort or nausea with exertion, particularly in inferior-territory ischaemia involving the right or circumflex coronary arteries.
Dizziness or light-headedness during exertion associated with ischaemia-induced reduction in cardiac output.
Diaphoresis during anginal episodes in high-grade ischaemia, reflecting sympathetic activation.
Symptoms that resolve completely within 5-10 minutes of rest or within 3 minutes of sublingual glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) — this response pattern distinguishes angina from non-cardiac pain.
Jaw tightening or toothache-like ache during brisk walking in the absence of dental pathology, a classic anginal equivalent particularly common in men over 55.
earlyWarningSigns: First episode of exertional chest discomfort lasting 2-5 minutes that resolves at rest — warrants cardiac evaluation within 1-2 weeks even if symptoms have fully resolved.
Previously stable exertional threshold deteriorating over 1-2 weeks: symptoms now provoked at lower workload or persisting slightly longer — unstable angina until proven otherwise.
New unexplained exertional breathlessness or fatigue in a person with known coronary risk factors without prior cardiac diagnosis.
Resting ECG showing new ST depression, T-wave inversion, or Q-waves in the context of new chest symptoms in the previous 48-72 hours.
emergencySigns: Chest pain at rest lasting more than 15-20 minutes not relieved by two doses of sublingual GTN — call emergency services; this is unstable angina or acute MI until excluded.
Syncope or pre-syncope accompanying chest pain — suggests severe ischaemia, arrhythmia, or haemodynamic compromise requiring immediate assessment.
New diaphoresis, pallor, and breathlessness at rest with chest discomfort — signs of haemodynamic compromise; may indicate STEMI or large NSTEMI.
Chest pain with ECG showing new ST elevation in two or more contiguous leads — STEMI requiring immediate cath-lab activation regardless of symptom duration.

causes.description: The dominant cause of stable angina. Lipid-laden plaques narrow the coronary lumen by 50-70% or more, limiting the increase in coronary blood flow during exertion. Haemodynamically significant stenosis (>70% diameter reduction by angiography or FFR ≤0.80) restricts the flow reserve needed to meet increased myocardial demand during physical or emotional stress.

Focal or diffuse contraction of coronary smooth muscle transiently occludes or near-occludes the artery. The mechanism involves endothelial dysfunction, adrenergic hyperreactivity, and oxidative stress. Smoking, cocaine, amphetamines, cold exposure, and hyperventilation are established triggers. This underlies Prinzmetal's variant angina.

Structural and functional abnormalities of arterioles and capillaries (<300 µm) reduce coronary flow reserve measured by FFR or cardiac MRI stress. More prevalent in women, patients with hypertension, diabetes, and early atherosclerosis. Causes typical anginal symptoms with normal epicardial arteries.

Tachycardia (e.g., uncontrolled atrial fibrillation), severe hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, or thyrotoxicosis can precipitate angina across stenoses that are subcritical at rest. Managing the precipitant often relieves symptoms without revascularisation.

Unstable angina arises when a plaque fissures or erodes, causing platelet aggregation and partial thrombosis that dynamically narrows or transiently occludes the artery. Plaques responsible for unstable angina are often non-obstructive on baseline angiography before the event — underscoring the importance of plaque composition over stenosis severity.

riskFactors.description: Existing CAD is the proximate determinant of angina. Prior MI, prior PCI, or angiographic stenosis >50% in a major vessel places the patient in the highest-risk category for symptomatic ischaemia.
Systolic BP above 140 mmHg doubles coronary event risk and promotes LV hypertrophy, increasing resting myocardial oxygen demand and precipitating angina even across moderate stenoses.

LDL drives atherosclerotic plaque formation and endothelial dysfunction. Each 1 mmol/L LDL reduction reduces major coronary events by ~22% (CTT 2010). Reducing LDL below 1.4 mmol/L for secondary prevention also stabilises existing plaques.

Smoking accelerates plaque progression, promotes vasospasm, and impairs endothelial vasodilation. Current smokers have 2-4 times the angina burden of non-smokers; cessation halves recurrent anginal events within 1-2 years.

Diabetes promotes diffuse coronary artery disease and microvascular dysfunction. Diabetic patients often present with atypical or silent ischaemia; HbA1c above 9% doubles the hazard for angina progression.
Coronary atherosclerosis accumulates over decades; stable angina prevalence rises from approximately 5% at age 45-54 to 15% at age 65-74 in both sexes.
Men develop obstructive CAD approximately 10 years earlier than women. Post-menopausal women rapidly close the sex gap; microvascular angina is disproportionately more common in women of all ages.

affectedDemographics: Middle-aged to elderly adults: the majority of stable angina diagnoses are in individuals aged 55-75; prevalence rises steeply after age 55 in both sexes.
Post-menopausal women: accelerated CAD risk after menopause; women more often present with microvascular angina and atypical symptoms, leading to underdiagnosis.
Patients with prior revascularisation: approximately 15-25% of patients who have undergone PCI or CABG develop recurrent angina within 3-5 years from progression or in-stent restenosis.
South Asian populations: higher premature CAD prevalence and a lower angina symptom threshold relative to degree of stenosis compared with European populations.
Patients with type 2 diabetes: higher prevalence of silent or atypical ischaemia; angina is twice as common compared with matched non-diabetic populations.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of angina begins with a structured history using the Diamond-Forrester classification: typical angina (central chest discomfort with exertion relieved by rest or nitrate in all three dimensions) has a high pre-test probability of CAD and may not require further non-invasive testing before referral for angiography in older men. Atypical angina (two of three features) and non-anginal chest pain have lower pre-test probability, guiding investigation intensity. The resting 12-lead ECG is obtained in all patients; it is normal in more than 50% of stable angina patients but may show LV hypertrophy, prior Q-waves, or ST-T changes that shift probability. Exercise ECG stress testing at 85% of target heart rate has sensitivity of 68% and specificity of 77% for detecting obstructive CAD; it provides prognostic information via Duke Treadmill Score. Myocardial perfusion scintigraphy (MPS), stress echocardiography, and stress cardiac MRI have higher diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity 80-90%) and are preferred in patients with resting ECG abnormalities, previous revascularisation, or intermediate probability. CT coronary angiography (CTCA) has sensitivity of 95-99% for excluding obstructive CAD (ESC 2019 chronic coronary syndrome guidelines recommend CTCA as first-line for symptomatic stable chest pain of low-to-intermediate probability). Invasive coronary angiography with fractional flow reserve (FFR ≤0.80) confirms haemodynamic significance of stenoses and guides revascularisation. In women and patients with suspected microvascular angina, adenosine-stress cardiac MRI or coronary flow reserve measurement with pressure wire is the most accurate investigation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies prior MI (Q-waves), LV hypertrophy, bundle branch block, or ischaemic changes; determines baseline for comparison during symptoms
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10 electrodes placed on the chest and limbs; takes 1 minute. No preparation. Normal result in >50% of stable angina patients — a normal ECG does not exclude angina.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive anatomical assessment of coronary artery stenosis; recommended first-line for stable chest pain of low-to-intermediate probability (ESC 2019)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Intravenous contrast is injected via a small cannula; the scanner takes images in 5-10 minutes with brief breath-holds. Iodine contrast allergy must be disclosed. Results within 24-48 hours. Radiation dose approximately 2-4 mSv.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provokes ischaemia at peak exercise workload; provides Duke Treadmill Score for prognostication
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Walking on a treadmill at increasing speed and incline for up to 12 minutes while ECG and blood pressure are monitored. Requires ability to exercise and normal baseline ECG. ST depression of ≥1 mm at peak exercise is a positive result.

diagnosticTests.test: Stress echocardiography or stress cardiac MRI
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects inducible wall-motion abnormality or perfusion defect; higher sensitivity than exercise ECG
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: For stress echo: echocardiogram at rest then during peak exercise or IV dobutamine infusion — 30-45 minutes total. Stress MRI uses IV adenosine or regadenoson and takes 45-60 minutes lying in the MRI bore.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises coronary anatomy; FFR ≤0.80 confirms haemodynamically significant stenosis and guides PCI decision
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Radial artery puncture under local anaesthesia; catheters advanced to coronary ostia; contrast injected under X-ray guidance. Procedure 30-60 minutes. FFR wire crosses stenosis to measure pressure drop. Day-case procedure; patient goes home 4-6 hours later.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes unstable angina (normal troponin) from NSTEMI (troponin above 99th percentile URL)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn at 0 and 1-3 hours; results within 30-60 minutes. Normal troponin on two serial samples effectively rules out NSTEMI but does not exclude significant ischaemia.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of angina pursues two goals simultaneously: preventing MI and death (prognostic treatment) and relieving symptoms (anti-anginal treatment). First-line prognostic therapy for all angina patients with underlying CAD follows the 2019 ESC and 2021 ACC/AHA chronic coronary syndrome guidelines: aspirin 75-100 mg daily, high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80 mg or rosuvastatin 40 mg) targeting LDL below 1.4 mmol/L, and an ACE inhibitor for patients with diabetes, LV dysfunction, or hypertension. First-line anti-anginal therapy is a beta-blocker (metoprolol, bisoprolol) or a rate-lowering calcium channel blocker (verapamil, diltiazem) to reduce heart rate and myocardial oxygen demand; long-acting nitrates or ivabradine may be added as second-line agents. Sublingual GTN (glyceryl trinitrate 0.4 mg spray or 0.3-0.6 mg tablet) is prescribed to all patients for acute symptom relief. Ranolazine (500-1000 mg twice daily) reduces late sodium current in ischaemic cardiomyocytes and is an effective add-on agent approved for chronic angina when standard agents are insufficient or not tolerated. For vasospastic angina, calcium channel blockers are first-line with long-acting nitrates added second; beta-blockers are generally avoided as they may worsen spasm. Coronary revascularisation via PCI or CABG relieves angina in 80-90% of appropriately selected patients and is recommended when symptoms are inadequately controlled on optimal medical therapy or when anatomy predicts prognostic benefit (left main, proximal LAD, or three-vessel disease). The ORBITA trial demonstrated that PCI provides meaningful angina symptom relief over placebo even in carefully blinded patients. Lifestyle modification — smoking cessation, regular aerobic exercise, weight management — is integral at every stage.

medicalTreatments.name: Beta-blocker (bisoprolol 5-10 mg daily or metoprolol succinate 50-200 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces heart rate and myocardial oxygen demand by blocking sympathetic beta-1 receptors. First-line anti-anginal agent; also prognostic in patients with LV dysfunction or prior MI. Titrate to resting HR 55-65 bpm.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces anginal episodes by 50-70% in comparative trials; reduces exercise-induced ST depression; reduces mortality post-MI by ~23%

medicalTreatments.name: Sublingual glyceryl trinitrate (GTN 0.4 mg spray or 0.3-0.6 mg tablet)
medicalTreatments.description: Venodilator reducing preload and relieving acute anginal episodes within 1-3 minutes. Every patient with angina must carry GTN at all times. If pain persists after two doses 5 minutes apart, emergency services should be called.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Relieves acute angina in over 90% of episodes within 3-5 minutes; onset of action 1-2 minutes

medicalTreatments.name: Long-acting calcium channel blocker (amlodipine 5-10 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces systemic vascular resistance, blood pressure, and coronary arterial tone. Preferred over beta-blockers in vasospastic angina and as alternative first-line agent when beta-blockers are contraindicated (asthma, severe bradycardia).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces anginal frequency by approximately 50% in stable CAD; reduces vascular events in hypertensive patients with coronary disease (CAMELOT trial)

medicalTreatments.name: Aspirin (75-100 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Irreversible COX-1 inhibitor blocking platelet thromboxane production. Reduces MI risk in all patients with established CAD. Continued indefinitely unless contraindicated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces major cardiovascular events by 25% in patients with established vascular disease (Antithrombotic Trialists Collaboration)

medicalTreatments.name: High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80 mg or rosuvastatin 40 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces LDL cholesterol, stabilises atherosclerotic plaques, and lowers recurrent ischaemic events. Target LDL <1.4 mmol/L for established CAD per ESC 2021 guidelines. Started regardless of baseline LDL.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Each 1 mmol/L LDL reduction cuts major vascular events by ~22% per year; high-intensity statins reduce events by 35-40% relative to placebo in primary and secondary prevention

medicalTreatments.name: Ranolazine (500-1000 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Inhibits late inward sodium current reducing ischaemia-induced intracellular calcium overload. Add-on agent for refractory angina when beta-blocker or CCB inadequately controls symptoms. Does not affect heart rate or blood pressure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: CARISA and ERICA trials: reduced weekly anginal episodes by 1.5-2.1 vs placebo (p<0.001); increased exercise duration by ~45 seconds

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous Coronary Intervention (PCI) with drug-eluting stent
surgicalOptions.description: Coronary angioplasty and stenting of the culprit stenosis under local anaesthesia. Recommended when symptoms are inadequately controlled on optimal medical therapy and anatomy is suitable for PCI. ORBITA-2 confirmed clinically meaningful symptom benefit.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Angina freedom at 12 months in 70-80% of stable angina patients after PCI; 5-year angina freedom approximately 65%; restenosis rate <10% with current-generation DES

surgicalOptions.name: Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting (CABG)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical revascularisation preferred for left main disease, three-vessel disease with diabetes (FREEDOM trial, PMID 22897701), or when PCI is technically not feasible. Left internal mammary artery graft to LAD gives survival advantage over PCI in multivessel disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic relief in 85-90% at 1 year; 10-year freedom from angina approximately 50-60%; 10-year LIMA-LAD patency >90%

alternativeTreatments.name: Cardiac rehabilitation and supervised exercise training
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured aerobic exercise programmes improve coronary collateral flow, endothelial function, and anginal threshold. Meta-analyses show exercise-based rehabilitation reduces angina frequency and improves quality of life comparably to PCI in stable multivessel disease (COURAGE trial, PMID 17387127).

alternativeTreatments.name: Enhanced external counterpulsation (EECP)
alternativeTreatments.description: Pneumatic leg compression timed to the cardiac cycle augments diastolic coronary perfusion. FDA-approved for refractory stable angina; reduces anginal frequency by ~2 episodes per week in randomised MUST-EECP trial (PMID 10367210).

whySeeSpecialist: A cardiologist should be seen for any new chest pain with exertional pattern, any episode of rest angina, or worsening anginal threshold over days to weeks. Interventional cardiology input is needed when symptoms persist on optimal medical therapy or when anatomy assessment for revascularisation is required.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a board-certified cardiologist with access to CT coronary angiography, stress imaging, and invasive catheterisation within the same network. For complex multivessel disease, seek assessment at a centre that holds weekly multidisciplinary Heart Team meetings to select between PCI and CABG.
hospitalCriteria: Access to CT coronary angiography for outpatient evaluation
Invasive catheterisation laboratory with FFR capability
Multidisciplinary Heart Team (interventional cardiologist + cardiac surgeon) for complex cases
24/7 cath-lab activation for acute decompensation to unstable angina
Cardiac rehabilitation programme for post-revascularisation patients
keyFacilities: Chest pain assessment units with access to stress imaging
Tertiary cardiac centres with high-volume PCI and CABG programmes
Lipid and cardiovascular risk clinics for secondary prevention optimisation
preventionStrategies: Achieve and maintain LDL cholesterol below 2.6 mmol/L for primary prevention and below 1.4 mmol/L for established CAD using statins; adding ezetimibe or a PCSK9 inhibitor when statin alone is insufficient.
Control blood pressure to below 130/80 mmHg using lifestyle and antihypertensive therapy to slow plaque progression.
Stop smoking: coronary event risk falls by 50% within one year of cessation and reaches near-baseline within five years.
Exercise at least 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity weekly; physical inactivity doubles coronary event risk.
Manage diabetes with glycaemic targets agreed with the treating physician; GLP-1 receptor agonists and SGLT2 inhibitors provide additional cardiovascular benefit in high-risk patients.
Screen for and treat obstructive sleep apnoea; untreated OSA increases nocturnal ischaemic burden and worsens anginal threshold.
lifestyleModifications: Always carry sublingual GTN spray or tablets and use promptly when angina begins; sit down, spray under the tongue, and wait 5 minutes before a second dose.
Avoid anginal triggers where identified: cold air, heavy meals immediately before exercise, emotional stress, and extreme temperature changes.
Exercise regularly within the prescribed anginal threshold; supervised cardiac rehabilitation improves exercise tolerance and collateral coronary flow.
Maintain healthy weight; each 5-unit increase in BMI above 25 independently increases anginal symptom burden.
Limit alcohol to under 14 units per week; excess alcohol raises blood pressure and can trigger arrhythmia.
Manage psychological stress with relaxation techniques or cognitive behavioural therapy; emotional triggers provoke 30-50% of anginal episodes in ambulatory monitoring studies.

recommended: Mediterranean diet pattern (olive oil, fish, vegetables, legumes, nuts) reduces recurrent coronary events by approximately 30% (PREDIMED trial)
Oily fish (2+ portions per week) for omega-3 fatty acids reducing triglycerides and platelet reactivity
Whole grains and high-fibre carbohydrates reducing LDL and improving glycaemic control
Fresh vegetables and fruit providing potassium, antioxidants, and nitrate-rich leafy greens that support vascular function
avoid: Saturated and trans fats raising LDL cholesterol and accelerating plaque formation
High-sodium foods worsening hypertension and fluid retention in those with LV dysfunction
Large meals immediately before exertion, which divert blood flow to the gut and can precipitate post-prandial angina
Excess caffeine (>400 mg/day) which raises heart rate and may precipitate angina in susceptible individuals

exerciseGuidelines: Aerobic exercise within the anginal threshold is beneficial and not contraindicated. Target 150 minutes per week of moderate activity (e.g., brisk walking at a pace at which conversation is possible). Stop immediately if exertional chest discomfort begins and use GTN spray if not resolved by rest. Avoid isometric exercises (heavy lifting) and sudden high-intensity bursts until cleared by a cardiologist. Formal exercise stress testing guides safe exercise prescription.
dailyManagement: Take all prescribed medications at consistent times; do not omit beta-blockers abruptly (rebound ischaemia and MI risk).
Carry GTN at all times; check expiry date every 3-6 months as open tablets lose potency after 8 weeks.
Record anginal episodes in a diary — frequency, duration, provoking activity, and GTN response — to detect deterioration in threshold.
Attend annual or biannual cardiologist review for lipid, BP, and glycaemic targets reassessment and consideration of stress imaging.
Monitor weight and fluid balance; unexplained weight gain of >2 kg in 48 hours may signal cardiac decompensation.
Report new rest pain, nocturnal angina, or angina requiring more than two GTN doses to the clinical team the same day.
prognosis: Stable angina on optimal medical therapy carries an annual major cardiovascular event rate (MI or cardiac death) of approximately 1-2%. Five-year mortality is approximately 5-8% in trials of stable CAD patients without LV dysfunction. Prognosis depends on coronary anatomy — proximal LAD or left main disease carries a 5-year mortality of 10-15% versus under 5% for single-vessel non-proximal disease. LVEF is the dominant prognostic variable: LVEF below 35% raises 2-year mortality to approximately 20% even with optimal therapy. Complete revascularisation improves prognosis in three-vessel disease and left main disease compared with medical therapy alone (SYNTAX trial, PMID 19228612). Unstable angina without troponin rise carries a 30-day MI or death risk of 5-10% on modern anti-thrombotic therapy. Patients who complete cardiac rehabilitation after revascularisation have approximately 25% lower all-cause mortality at 5 years than those who do not.
recoveryTimeline: After PCI: discharge same day or next day; return to desk work within 1-2 weeks; graded return to manual work within 4-6 weeks. After CABG: hospital stay 5-7 days; return to light activities at 4-6 weeks; full recovery 3-6 months. On optimal medical therapy without revascularisation: symptom stabilisation expected within 4-8 weeks of starting full-dose beta-blocker and statin; lifestyle changes take 3-6 months for full benefit.
complications: Acute myocardial infarction: unstable angina evolves to NSTEMI or STEMI in approximately 10-20% of cases if left untreated; stable angina patients have a 1-2% annual MI risk.
Heart failure from ischaemic cardiomyopathy: recurrent ischaemia damages myocardial contractility; LVEF below 40% develops in approximately 20% of patients with multi-vessel disease within 5 years.
Ventricular arrhythmias: acute ischaemia during angina episodes can trigger VT/VF, accounting for a proportion of angina-related sudden cardiac deaths.
In-stent restenosis (post-PCI): occurs in approximately 5-10% with current-generation drug-eluting stents within 12 months, requiring repeat revascularisation.
Psychological impact: chronic angina causes anxiety and depression in 25-35% of patients, significantly impairing quality of life and adherence to therapy.
Surgical complications of CABG: stroke (1-3%), atrial fibrillation (25-30%), wound infection (2-5%), and renal impairment in patients with pre-existing chronic kidney disease.

supportResources.name: American Heart Association — Angina Information
supportResources.description: Patient guides on angina types, GTN use, activity guidelines, and when to call emergency services.

supportResources.name: British Heart Foundation — Angina
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient information on angina management, medication, and cardiac rehabilitation in the UK.

supportResources.name: ESC Patient Resources — Chronic Coronary Syndromes
supportResources.description: European Society of Cardiology patient-facing resources on stable angina, lifestyle, and treatment options.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute MI causes prolonged chest pain lasting more than 20 minutes at rest with troponin rise above the 99th-percentile URL, confirming irreversible myocardial necrosis. Stable angina produces transient exertional discomfort resolving within 5-10 minutes with normal troponin. Unstable angina without troponin rise shares some features with NSTEMI and requires the same urgent work-up.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Pericarditis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pericarditis causes sharp pleuritic pain worsened by inspiration and lying flat, improved by leaning forward, with diffuse saddle-shaped ST elevation on ECG across multiple leads and a pericardial friction rub. Angina produces pressure-like exertional discomfort in a coronary-territory ECG distribution, relieved by nitrates.

confusedWithConditions.name: Thoracic Aortic Dissection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Aortic dissection presents with acute tearing or ripping pain of maximal intensity at onset, often migrating from chest to back, with unequal arm blood pressures and mediastinal widening. Angina is effort-related, subsides at rest, and does not migrate. Nitrates are potentially harmful in dissection as they reduce aortic pressure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Exertional dyspnoea and chest tightness in asthma are accompanied by wheeze, prolonged expiration, and peak-flow reduction, and respond to bronchodilators rather than GTN. In angina equivalent presentations without chest pain, spirometry and exercise ECG help differentiate the two conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Coronary Artery Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Angina is the primary symptomatic manifestation of obstructive CAD; treating the underlying atherosclerosis is central to both symptom relief and prognosis.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
relatedConditions.relevance: Unstable angina lies on the same ACS spectrum as MI; plaque rupture without occlusive thrombus in unstable angina can progress to MI within hours or days if untreated.

relatedConditions.name: Heart Failure
relatedConditions.relevance: Ischaemic cardiomyopathy from recurrent ischaemia and unrevascularised multivessel disease is the most common cause of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.

relatedConditions.name: Peripheral Artery Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Atherosclerosis is a systemic disease; 30-40% of patients with symptomatic peripheral artery disease have concomitant significant CAD, and vice versa.

faqs.question: What does angina feel like?
faqs.answer: Angina typically feels like central chest tightness, pressure, or heaviness, often described as a heavy weight or squeezing sensation across the chest. Discomfort may radiate to the left arm, jaw, or back. It usually begins during exertion or stress, lasts 2-10 minutes, and resolves completely with rest or a sublingual nitrate spray.

faqs.question: Is angina the same as a heart attack?
faqs.answer: No. Angina is caused by temporary myocardial ischaemia without cell death; troponin is normal, symptoms resolve within 10 minutes, and the heart muscle recovers fully. A heart attack (MI) causes permanent myocardial necrosis: pain lasts more than 20 minutes, troponin rises above normal, and the damage is irreversible. Unstable angina shares features with NSTEMI and is treated as an emergency.

faqs.question: What triggers angina?
faqs.answer: Stable angina is triggered by anything that increases the heart's oxygen demand: physical exertion (particularly climbing stairs or walking uphill), cold air, heavy meals, emotional stress, or sexual activity. Vasospastic angina typically occurs at rest, often at night or in the early morning. Most triggers are consistent and predictable for a given patient.

faqs.question: How is angina treated?
faqs.answer: All angina patients need aspirin, a high-intensity statin, and a sublingual GTN spray for acute attacks. Regular anti-anginal medications — beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, long-acting nitrates, or ranolazine — reduce attack frequency. When symptoms persist on optimal medical therapy, coronary angiography with PCI or CABG relieves angina in 80-90% of appropriately selected patients.

faqs.question: What is unstable angina?
faqs.answer: Unstable angina is chest pain occurring at rest, with minimal exertion, or with a pattern that is worsening rapidly (new onset, increasing frequency, or lower exertional threshold), with normal troponin levels. It represents plaque instability and carries a 5-10% risk of MI within 30 days without urgent treatment. It requires hospital admission and anti-thrombotic therapy.

faqs.question: How does GTN (nitrate spray) work for angina?
faqs.answer: Sublingual glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) releases nitric oxide, dilating coronary arteries and peripheral veins within 1-3 minutes. Venodilation reduces preload and myocardial oxygen demand, relieving ischaemia and pain. It relieves acute angina in over 90% of episodes. GTN should be used sitting down to prevent fainting from blood pressure drop.

faqs.question: Can angina be cured?
faqs.answer: Angina from obstructive CAD is not cured but can be fully controlled. Coronary revascularisation (PCI or CABG) eliminates symptoms in 80-90% of appropriate patients. Optimal medical therapy with statins, antiplatelets, and anti-anginal drugs substantially reduces attacks and lowers MI risk. Vasospastic angina often responds completely to calcium channel blockers.

faqs.question: What is microvascular angina?
faqs.answer: Microvascular angina (also called cardiac syndrome X) occurs when typical anginal symptoms arise from coronary microvascular dysfunction despite normal epicardial arteries on angiography. It is diagnosed with coronary flow reserve measurement or stress cardiac MRI. More common in women, it is treated with beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, and ranolazine rather than PCI.

faqs.question: How is angina diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis begins with history and resting ECG. CT coronary angiography is recommended first-line for stable chest pain of low-to-intermediate probability (ESC 2019). Stress testing (exercise ECG, stress echo, or stress MRI) identifies inducible ischaemia. Invasive angiography with FFR confirms haemodynamic significance and guides revascularisation.

faqs.question: Is angina dangerous?
faqs.answer: Stable angina on optimal medical therapy carries an annual MI or cardiac death rate of approximately 1-2%. Unstable angina is more dangerous: without treatment the 30-day MI or death risk is 5-10%. The long-term prognosis depends on coronary anatomy, LV ejection fraction, and adherence to secondary prevention therapy.

faqs.question: Can women have angina without chest pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Women with angina more often present with atypical symptoms such as breathlessness, jaw pain, upper back discomfort, nausea, or unexplained fatigue rather than classic central chest pressure. This leads to frequent underdiagnosis; women with these exertional symptoms and coronary risk factors should be investigated for ischaemia with the same urgency as men.

faqs.question: What is the difference between stable and unstable angina?
faqs.answer: Stable angina is predictable: symptoms appear consistently at similar exertional levels and always resolve within 10 minutes at rest or with GTN. Unstable angina is unpredictable: symptoms occur at rest, with minimal effort, or are escalating rapidly over days. Unstable angina is a medical emergency because it signals imminent plaque rupture and MI risk.

faqs.question: Can exercise help angina?
faqs.answer: Yes. Supervised cardiac rehabilitation exercise reduces anginal frequency and improves exercise tolerance. Regular moderate aerobic exercise improves endothelial function, raises the ischaemic threshold, and promotes collateral coronary vessel development. Exercise is performed within the anginal threshold — stopping immediately if symptoms begin — and guided by a stress test.

faqs.question: What should I do during an angina attack?
faqs.answer: Sit or stand still immediately and rest. Use sublingual GTN spray (one spray under the tongue); repeat after 5 minutes if symptoms persist. If pain is not fully gone after two doses or lasts more than 15 minutes, call emergency services — this may be a heart attack. Do not drive yourself to hospital.

faqs.question: Does angina always mean coronary artery disease?
faqs.answer: Not always. Vasospastic angina and microvascular angina produce typical anginal symptoms without obstructive epicardial CAD on angiography. Other conditions causing supply-demand mismatch — severe aortic stenosis, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and severe anemia — can produce anginal symptoms with normal coronary arteries.

faqs.question: Can angina go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Stable angina symptoms may improve with aggressive risk factor modification, smoking cessation, weight loss, and exercise over 6-12 months, partly through plaque stabilisation and improved endothelial function. However, the underlying coronary artery disease persists and MI risk remains if statins and antiplatelets are not taken. Spontaneous resolution should not delay investigation.

simpleName: Angina (chest pain from reduced heart blood flow)
metaDescription: Angina pectoris: chest pain from coronary blood flow restriction. Types, symptoms, GTN use, diagnosis with CTCA, and treatment including PCI reviewed.
